Species Distribution Modeling from Remot: This domain covers computational methods for predicting where species occur by combining remote sensing imagery, citizen science observations, and machine learning, including foundational tools like MaxEnt and reviews for ML practitioners. Readings span deep learning approaches using Sentinel-2 time-series (e.g., for orchids and birds in SatBird), heterogeneous graph neural networks, and specialized techniques such as imbalance-aware presence-only loss functions and predicting occurrence from partial observations. Together they address scalability, biodiversity monitoring in the Anthropocene, and the practical challenges of modeling species distributions globally.
Biodiversity Data Quality & Labeling for: This domain examines the reliability and cleanliness of datasets used to train conservation AI, exposing how benchmarks like ImageNet misrepresent biodiversity and how citizen-science images contain bugs and errors. Readings explore domain-specific distribution shifts in volunteer-collected data (DivShift), classification under ground-truth uncertainty in camera traps, and efficient labeling via data programming on geospatial information (GeoLabels). The focus is on building robust models by improving data curation and handling label noise.
Automated Wildlife Monitoring via Sensor: This domain focuses on continuous, automated tracking of wildlife using acoustic sensors and on-edge computing, including agile modeling workflows for bioacoustic monitoring. Readings address running energy-efficient deep learning models on edge devices for critter monitoring and long-term observation of bird flocks in the wild. The emphasis is on practical, scalable sensor-based approaches to biodiversity surveillance.
Forest & Vegetation Monitoring from Sate: This domain covers remote sensing techniques for mapping and characterizing forests and vegetation, using satellite, UAV, and LiDAR data to estimate tree diversity, classify species, and detect canopy changes. Readings include spectral variability approaches for tropical forests, multi-temporal NDVI analysis of California floristic richness, multimodal and few-shot segmentation methods, and fine-tuning foundation models like EarthPT for tree canopy mapping. Applications range from detecting human-driven forest alterations to fine-resolution vegetation monitoring from space.
Anti-Poaching & Illegal-Activity Detecti: This domain applies AI and remote sensing to detect and predict illegal activities threatening wildlife, such as poaching and illegal cattle ranching visible in high-resolution satellite imagery. Readings include generative AI methods for poaching prediction (latent composite flow matching), weakly-semi-supervised object detection, and active learning with multimodal imagery to locate rhino habitats. The focus is on proactive conservation enforcement through detection and prediction.
Phenology & Ecological Response to Clima: This domain studies the timing of biological events like flowering and dormancy and how species respond to climate, using drone imagery, satellite sensing, and deep learning. Readings cover cost-effective flowering monitoring of tropical trees, hybrid models for temperature effects on tree dormancy, buffelgrass green-up prediction, insect range shifts via out-of-distribution detection, and wildflower phenology from expert-annotated images. The emphasis is on tracking and forecasting ecological responses to a changing climate.
Wildfire & Climate Hazard Prediction fro: This domain leverages remote sensing and deep learning to map, detect, and predict wildfires and related climate hazards, including burned area mapping and short-term fire hazard forecasting. Readings address semantic segmentation of active fire on unbalanced classes, semi-supervised domain adaptation for wildfire detection, prototype-oriented change detection for disaster management, and predicting concurrent heatwaves, droughts, and wildfires. The focus is on hazard prediction and disaster response, with some related SAR-based detection methods.
Community-Engaged & Indigenous-Led Conse: This domain highlights conservation efforts that center local communities, Indigenous knowledge, and human-in-the-loop collaboration, including media monitoring for low-resource languages and managing wild salmon fisheries in Indigenous rivers. Readings blend technical multimodal AI approaches with narrative conservation stories such as saving jungle habitats and rescuing 'dancing' bears. The emphasis is on culturally grounded, participatory approaches to environmental stewardship.
Aquatic & Freshwater Ecosystem Conservat: This domain addresses the monitoring and protection of aquatic and freshwater ecosystems, from predicting seagrass ecosystem states with graph neural networks to building datasets for aquatic invertebrates in Vietnam. Readings also include global optimization methods for conserving freshwater fishes under the UN's 30x30 goal. The focus spans marine and freshwater biodiversity, spanning data collection, ecosystem state prediction, and conservation planning.
Conservation Foundation Models & Transfe: This domain develops general-purpose, pretrained models and embeddings that transfer across ecological and geospatial tasks, including climatic implicit embeddings (Climplicit) and pretrained geographic location encoders. Readings cover masked autoencoders for reconstructing remote sensing data (CuMoLoS-MAE) and fine-tuning language models for biodiversity named entity recognition to curate environmental knowledge graphs. The emphasis is on reusable, transferable representations that support diverse conservation applications.
AI-based human-wildlife conflict monitor: This domain focuses on using AI and media analysis to detect and understand human-wildlife conflicts, including few-shot approaches for environmental conservation media monitoring in low-resource languages. It covers combining news articles and aerial imagery to identify conflict hotspots, as demonstrated in the Indian context, and extends to interpreting public perception of weather disasters through metaphor analysis. Together, these readings emphasize leveraging textual and visual data sources to monitor and interpret human-environment tensions.
Digitizing indigenous ecological knowled: This domain addresses the capture and digitization of indigenous knowledge to strengthen community resilience against climate change. It explores multimodal foundation AI paired with expert-in-the-loop methods for sustainably managing wild salmon fisheries in indigenous rivers, as well as efforts to rescue and preserve endangered cultural and ecological practices. The readings collectively highlight how technology can support and safeguard traditional ecological knowledge for climate adaptation.
